content.description: Relative clauses give us extra information about something/someone or identify which particular thing/person we are talking about. They are often introduced by the following words.

rows.label: which (for things and animals)
items: Did you see the match **which** was shown live on TV last night?

rows.label: who (for people, and animals when we want to give them a personality)
items: Paula Radcliffe, **who** is competing in the marathon in Berlin this month, is my guest today.

rows.label: when (for times)
items: Do you remember the day **when** we won the championship?

rows.label: where (for places)
items: This is the stadium **where** they held the Olympic finals.

rows.label: why (for reasons)
items: That's the reason **why** she trains so hard every day.

rows.label: whom (for people as the object of the relative clause)
items: Is that the coach **whom** we met at the training camp last week?

rows.label: whose (for possession)
items: My next guest is Rafael Nadal, **whose** career stretches back to the early two-thousands.

items.note: When the relative pronoun (*who*, *which*, etc) is the subject of the relative clause, you do **not** need another subject.

items.correct: I admire Cristiano Ronaldo, **who** always pushes himself to the limit in training.

items.incorrect: I admire Cristiano Ronaldo, **who he** always pushes himself to the limit in training.

items.note: *Whom* is quite formal. It is natural in informal English to use *who* instead of *whom*, even when it is the object of the relative clause. After a preposition, however, we always use *whom*. Informally, we usually put the preposition at the end of the clause and use *who*.

items.correct: Is that the sprinter **who** we saw at the national championships?
Usain Bolt was a sprint legend **to whom** all younger athletes owe a great deal.
Usain Bolt was a sprint legend **who** all younger athletes owe a great deal **to**.

items.note: *Where* can be replaced by a preposition + *which*. Less formally, we can put the preposition at the end of the clause.

items.correct: The stadium **where / in which** I first competed is somewhere around here.
The stadium **which** I first competed **in** is somewhere around here.

items.note: We can do the same thing with *when*.

items.correct: Do you know the year **when / in which** the first modern Olympics was held?
Do you know the year **which** the first modern Olympics was held **in**?

content.description: Non-defining relative clauses simply give us more information about something/someone. The sentence makes complete sense without the relative clause.

rows.label: To give extra information about something/someone
items: Mia Santos, **who finished first in** the 5K race, is now training for the national championships.

items.note: Non-defining relative clauses are separated from the rest of the sentence by commas.

items.correct: The marathon, *which was run in perfect weather conditions*, was a truly thrilling event.

items.note: We cannot leave out the word which introduces the relative clause and we cannot use the word *that* instead.

items.incorrect: The marathon, was run in perfect weather conditions, was a truly thrilling event.
The marathon, *that* was run in perfect weather conditions, was a truly thrilling event.

items.note: *Which* can refer back to the whole of the sentence.

items.correct: We finally got tickets for the championship final, **which** was very lucky. *(It doesn't mean the final was lucky. It means getting tickets for the final was lucky.)*

content.description: Defining relative clauses tell us which one of a group of things/people we are talking about. The sentence doesn't usually make complete sense without the relative clause.

rows.label: To tell us which one of a group of things/people we are talking about
items: The training programme **which** I've been following has made a real difference.
The one **who** is wearing a red bib is the club champion, isn't it?

items.note: Defining relative clauses are not separated from the rest of the sentence by commas.

items.correct: *This is the medal which I told you about the other day.*

items.note: We can also use *that* to introduce the relative clause.

items.correct: *This is the medal **that** I told you about the other day.*

items.note: We can often leave out the word which introduces the relative clause when it is the object of the clause.

items.correct: *This is the medal I told you about the other day.*

items.note: Notice that we do not need a preposition when we use *where* or *when*.

items.correct: *The gym **where** I first trained is somewhere around here.*

items.incorrect: *The gym **where** I first trained **in** is somewhere around here.*

items.correct: *Do you know the year **when** the first modern marathon was run?*

items.incorrect: *Do you know the year **when** the first modern marathon was run **in**?*

content.description:
Present participles end in *-ing*.
Past participles usually end in *-ed*, although there are many irregular verbs.
Perfect participles are formed using *having* + past participle.

rows.type: To follow prepositions and conjunctions (present and perfect participles)
examples: By **appearing** in that sports drink advert, she risked losing her amateur status.
After **training / having trained** all morning, I was completely exhausted.

rows.type: To explain the reason for something (present and perfect participles)
examples: **Being** naturally competitive, Alex decided to pursue a career as a professional cyclist.
**Having watched** the race before, I knew exactly what was going to happen.

rows.type: To talk about actions happening at the same time (present participles)
examples: **Waiting** for the starting whistle, I felt incredibly nervous.

rows.type: To replace some relative clauses (present and past participles)
examples: Imagine being the person **coaching** a national football team! (= Imagine being the person *who is coaching* a national football team!)
The athlete **chosen** for the relay team will be notified by phone. (= The athlete *who is chosen* for the relay team will be notified by phone.)

rows.type: To talk about past actions happening in sequence (perfect participles)
examples: **Having finished** my workout, I decided to go for a swim.

rows.type: As an alternative passive form (past participles)
examples: **Made** to wait by the selectors, the young player began to feel frustrated.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: You have to be careful that the participle and the rest of the sentence both refer to the same subject.

items.correct: Watching the game, I saw a report about the new stadium.

items.incorrect: Watching the game, a report came on about the new stadium.

sections.title: A: Relative pronouns
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: The coach, **which / who** was appointed just last week, made no comment on the team's performance.
Isn't that the track **which / where** the world record was broken last summer?
The human heart, **which / who** beats about seventy times per minute at rest, works much harder during exercise.
There are several reasons **which / why** I don't want to train with that club tonight.
This is the gym **which / where** I work out every morning.
The new player in our squad, **who's / whose** name is Alexandra, seems really talented.
The Olympic torch, **which / that** people said could never go out, was extinguished during the relay.
All the athletes to **who / whom** the invitation was sent replied immediately.
The opening ceremony, **which / when** the host nation always enters last, is the highlight of the Olympics.
Andy Murray, **who / whose** new coaching team started work this week, is one of my favourite players.
A very popular sport is swimming, **which / who** is often recommended as a low-impact form of exercise.
Wembley Stadium, **which / that** was rebuilt and reopened in 2007, is one of the most famous venues in the world.

sections.title: B: Error correction (Relative clauses)
sections.instructions: Write OK if the bold word is correct. If it is incorrect, write the correct word.

items.sentence: The summer of 2012 was the year **which** London hosted the Olympic Games.
There were no energy drinks left at the aid station, **that** was really frustrating.
I've just bought some new running shoes **that** provide excellent grip on wet surfaces.
The marathon was won by Eliud Kipchoge, **who's** training methods have revolutionised the sport.
Can you think of any reason **which** the team captain should miss the final?
Here's a photo of the training camp where we **stayed in** over the summer.
How do you think the first athlete **whom** broke the four-minute mile felt?
This is the first occasion on **which** these two athletes have competed against each other.
He is a coach for **who** very few players feel much respect.
The moment **which** the sprinter crossed the finish line in world record time was breathtaking.
I trained with a coach **which** had worked with three Olympic champions.
My new running club, **who** I joined about three months ago, has been a wonderful experience.

sections.title: C: Rewrite as one sentence using a relative clause
sections.instructions: Rewrite as one complete sentence using a relative clause.

items.sentence: Usain Bolt is the fastest sprinter in history. His record in the 100m still stands.
items.answer: Usain Bolt, whose record in the 100m still stands, is the fastest sprinter in history.

items.sentence: The local sports club has started offering free coaching for children. That encourages many families.
items.answer: The local sports club has started offering free coaching for children, which encourages many families.

items.sentence: The marathon was first held as a modern Olympic event in 1896. It covers a distance of 42.195 kilometres.
items.answer: The marathon, which covers a distance of 42.195 kilometres, was first held as a modern Olympic event in 1896.

items.sentence: Wimbledon is one of the most prestigious tennis tournaments in the world. It has been played since 1877.
items.answer: Wimbledon, which has been played since 1877, is one of the most prestigious tennis tournaments in the world.

items.sentence: Swimming is an excellent form of exercise. It works almost every muscle group in the body.
items.answer: Swimming, which works almost every muscle group in the body, is an excellent form of exercise.

items.sentence: Basketball is popular in many countries around the world. It is played between two teams of five.
items.answer: Basketball, which is played between two teams of five, is popular in many countries around the world.

items.sentence: Parts of the national athletics stadium are open to the public. The Olympic team trains there.
items.answer: Parts of the national athletics stadium, where the Olympic team trains, are open to the public.

items.sentence: The cheetah is the fastest land animal. It can reach speeds of over 100 kilometres an hour.
items.answer: The cheetah, which can reach speeds of over 100 kilometres an hour, is the fastest land animal.

items.sentence: Pelé is considered one of the greatest footballers of all time. His real name was Edson Arantes do Nascimento.
items.answer: Pelé is considered one of the greatest footballers of all time, whose real name was Edson Arantes do Nascimento.

sections.title: D: Relative pronouns (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap. Do not write that and do not leave any gaps blank.

items.sentence: I'm sure there was a reason (1) ...... I became a sports journalist.
Maybe it was my love of athletics (2) ...... kept me entertained as a child.
Or maybe it was the athletes (3) ...... seemed to lead such exciting and adventurous lives.
I've covered so many matches lately (4) ...... have ended in disappointing draws that I've lost count.
Take the new fitness league, for example, the competition (5) ...... the federation hopes will boost participation in the sport.
The series features clubs (6) ...... managers have been replaced with younger coaches.
The problem with this format is that I'm just not sure at (7) ...... it's aimed – dedicated fans or casual followers.
Supporters (8) ...... follow this league will find it confusing, particularly at the moment (9) ...... the referees start making controversial decisions.
Younger fans (10) ...... watch more than the first few rounds will soon get bored with the managers, (11) ...... blame everyone but themselves for the poor results.
This is definitely one format (12) ...... should be scrapped immediately.
'Who's that near the finish line?' 'Oh, it's our new coach, ...... just joined the club this week.'
'Which race did you enter Ben in?' 'I entered him in the one ...... said he really wanted to compete in.'
'Who did you vote for as athlete of the year?' 'I'm not saying, but it was someone ...... name begins with "M".'
'Have you seen Leon Marsh's latest performance?' 'Is that the race in ...... breaks the course record?'
'Why do you rate that trainer so highly?' 'Well, she's one of the few coaches to ...... I can really listen.'
'Why don't we try the new sports centre this evening?' 'Is that the new facility ...... has just opened near the park?'
'Could you skip the training session today?' 'I'd like you to give me one good reason ...... I should.'
'Where do you want to run this weekend?' 'I think we should head somewhere ...... has good trails and open countryside.'

sections.title: F: Omit the relative pronoun
sections.instructions: There are 9 relative pronouns in the text which are not necessary. Click on them to mark them.
sections.passage: Fitness is one of the few hobbies [[that]] almost anyone can take up, regardless of age or experience. While there are professional coaches [[who]] we would all benefit from working with, many people prefer to train alone, using methods [[that]] they have discovered through online research. Discipline is a quality [[which]] is absolutely essential for any athlete hoping to reach their potential. Although many club members whose busy lives leave little time for regular training sometimes struggle to stay committed, the energy [[which]] sport gives you is truly remarkable. The improved mood [[which]] exercise brings is well known, and the personal records [[which]] runners hope to break and the goals [[that]] they set for each race give them a strong sense of purpose. Although there will always be people to whom competitive sport seems unnecessarily demanding, the determination it builds in young people cannot be overstated. Perseverance is a value [[which]] underpins the career of every successful athlete, and as long as proper support is offered, there will always be coaches [[who]] young people can look up to, as well as competitions [[that]] they can enter to stay motivated.

sections.title: G: Participles
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Before **meeting / met** your new coach, I had never understood the importance of a proper warm-up.
I'm sure that after **passing / passed** the fitness test, you'll feel a lot more confident.
Having **finishing / finished** her warm-up, Laura strapped on her running shoes.
**Looking / Looked** at the training schedule, I realised how demanding the week ahead would be.
I completed my workout and, **doing / having done** that, I went to get a protein shake.
**Hearing / Heard** the starting pistol, the athletes launched themselves off the blocks.
**Having lost / Losing** her racket, she was unable to practise for the match.
Having **been / Being** naturally athletic, Jade hopes to make the national squad.
**Having missed / Missing** the team briefing, I had no idea what tactics we were supposed to use.
After **making / made** his selection, the coach announced the starting line-up.

sections.title: H: Participle transformations
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences using a participle clause. Write the full sentence.

items.context: I spotted my coach's car in the car park while I was waiting for the bus to the training ground.
items.answer: for the bus to the training ground, I spotted my coach's car in the car park.

items.context: I pulled a muscle because I was trying to lift too much weight in the gym.
items.answer: to lift too much weight in the gym, I pulled a muscle.

items.context: I felt really inspired when I saw the young athletes compete at the regional championships.
items.answer: the young athletes compete at the regional championships, I felt really inspired.

items.context: Sara felt nervous because she knew the final was going to be incredibly tough.
items.answer: (that) the final was going to be incredibly tough, Sara felt nervous.

items.context: The reason I'm quite good at long-distance running is that I'm quite light on my feet.
items.answer: quite light on my feet, I'm quite good at long-distance running.

items.context: Tom didn't know the training route so he relied on a GPS watch when he ran there.
items.answer: knowing the training route, Tom relied on a GPS watch when he ran there.

items.context: Katie can afford to train at a professional sports facility because she has a sponsorship deal.
items.answer: a sponsorship deal, Katie can afford to train at a professional sports facility.

items.context: James began to slow down when he realised he had gone out too fast at the start of the race.
items.answer: (that) he had gone out too fast at the start of the race, James began to slow down.

sections.title: I: Error correction (Participles)
sections.instructions: Write OK if the sentence is correct. If it is incorrect, rewrite the full sentence correctly.

items.sentence: Looking at the gym timetable, a yoga class seemed to fit perfectly.
items.answer: Looking at the gym timetable, I saw that a yoga class would fit perfectly.

items.sentence: Realising she had forgotten her water bottle, she ran back to the changing room.
Having trained me for years, I was offered a place on the national team by the coach.
items.answer: Having trained me for years, the coach offered me a place on the national team.

items.sentence: Switching on the radio, the commentator was giving the team news for the final.
items.answer: Switching on the radio, I heard the commentator giving the team news for the final.

items.sentence: Picking up her gold medal, the champion smiled broadly and raised it above her head.
Having completed his warm-up, he felt confident and ready to compete.

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Each line either has one extra word that does not belong, or is correct. Click the extra word, or click OK if the line is correct.

items.sentence: Welcome to the Sports Arena, and welcome to the Champions League Final, the event who you
have been waiting for all season! Cast your mind back to last month, when our team captain,
Ben Harper, having been scored a hat-trick in the semi-final, vowed to lead the team to glory?
Well, here he is tonight, ready to lift the trophy, which it is the most coveted prize in club football.
Millions of fans are watching from around the world and if Ben can keep performing at this level, his team could be celebrating a historic victory tonight.
Having not played in a final of this magnitude, you can imagine just how
nerve-wracking which it must be out there. I know the whole squad is nervous, but I am sure they will be
absolutely magnificent, and there is one person for whom this occasion is deeply emotional, and that is Ben's
father, David, who he has been his biggest supporter since childhood. So, let the final begin!
